accepted_root_causes: Order-1 contiguous page allocation failure due to memory fragmentation (GFP_ATOMIC)
DMA32/Normal zones have free > min but zero contiguous 8kB+ blocks
High memory usage by nginx workers intensified fragmentation
action_guide: Increase vm.min_free_kbytes (e.g. 65536 or higher)
Reduce nginx worker count or connection limits
Review vm.zone_reclaim_mode settings
Verify memory compaction is enabled

accepted_root_causes: Order-4 contiguous page (64kB) allocation failure — Nvidia driver (nvidia_modeset) requested contiguous memory
136MB free in Normal zone but entirely in 4kB/8kB/16kB/32kB fragments — zero 64kB+ contiguous blocks
High anonymous memory usage by gnome-shell + firefox + code caused severe fragmentation
must_include_evidence: free:136156kB > min:30040kB (sufficient free memory but fragmented)
action_guide: Increase vm.min_free_kbytes significantly (131072 or higher)
Increase vm.compaction_proactiveness
Update Nvidia driver (GFP_ATOMIC to GFP_KERNEL patch)
Check Transparent Hugepage defrag settings
Limit firefox tab count or manage memory usage

accepted_root_causes: Order-2 contiguous page (16kB) allocation failure combined with no swap
Java process occupying ~84% of RAM leaving minimal free area with severe fragmentation
Normal zone free:42200kB near min:41280kB watermark, zero 16kB+ contiguous blocks
must_include_evidence: free:42200kB near min:41280kB (close to watermark threshold)
action_guide: Reduce JVM heap size (-Xmx)
Add swap space (create swap file on AWS)
Increase vm.min_free_kbytes
Consider upgrading instance memory

accepted_root_causes: Order-1 SLUB allocation failure during NVMe I/O writeback (GFP_ATOMIC|GFP_NOIO)
SLUB cache kmalloc-8192 completely exhausted (free: 0), no contiguous 8kB blocks available
PostgreSQL consuming ~4.7GB RSS on 16GB system left insufficient free pages for I/O path allocations
must_include_evidence: SLUB: Unable to allocate memory (kmalloc-8192, free: 0)
action_guide: Reduce PostgreSQL shared_buffers or work_mem
Increase vm.min_free_kbytes to reserve more free pages
Upgrade to RHEL 7.6+ where kswapd compaction is improved
Monitor slab usage with slabtop

accepted_root_causes: Order-1 GFP_ATOMIC allocation failure during TCP socket creation in GlusterFS replication path
Hyperconverged workload (GlusterFS + KVM VMs) consuming most of 32GB RAM, leaving fragmented free pages
Normal zone free:22400kB near min:21600kB with zero 16kB+ contiguous blocks
must_include_evidence: tcp_create_openreq_child in call trace (TCP socket allocation)
0*16kB in both DMA32 and Normal zones
free near min threshold in Normal zone
action_guide: Increase vm.min_free_kbytes (e.g. 131072 for 32GB system)
Separate GlusterFS and KVM workloads to different nodes
Upgrade to RHEL 7.4+ for kswapd compaction improvements
Consider dedicated network for GlusterFS replication traffic

accepted_root_causes: Order-1 page allocation failure on a small VM (2GB) with no swap
Python3 + Celery + Redis consuming nearly all available RAM
Both zones fragmented with zero 16kB+ contiguous blocks
action_guide: Add swap space to the VM
Reduce Python/Celery worker memory usage
Increase VM memory allocation
Set vm.min_free_kbytes appropriately for small VMs

accepted_root_causes: Order-3 contiguous page (32kB) allocation failure on a NUMA 2-node system
Both NUMA nodes have free memory but zero 64kB+ contiguous blocks due to severe fragmentation
MongoDB consuming ~24.8GB RSS on 64GB system plus Elasticsearch and logging agents filling remaining memory
must_include_evidence: 0*64kB on all three zones (no 64kB+ contiguous blocks)
mongod RSS ~24.8GB as primary memory consumer
action_guide: Configure MongoDB wiredTigerCacheSizeGB to limit RSS
Increase vm.min_free_kbytes (e.g. 262144 for 64GB NUMA system)
Set vm.zone_reclaim_mode=1 for NUMA-aware reclaim
Enable proactive compaction (vm.compaction_proactiveness)
Consider separating Elasticsearch to a different server

accepted_root_causes: The python process (PID 40639) hit the Docker container memory cgroup limit (524288kB = 512MB) and was killed by the cgroup OOM Killer. anon-rss (522280kB) nearly equals the limit, and failcnt 3779 indicates repeated memory allocation failures. mem_cgroup_out_of_memory in the Call Trace confirms this is a cgroup OOM, not a global OOM. The root cause is that PIL creates a raw pixel buffer of width×height×3 bytes during JPEG decoding, and additionally the original bytes, BytesIO buffer, and intermediate image objects are all held simultaneously, causing actual RSS to grow to over 3× the simple pixel calculation (6000*4500*3=81MB).
Docker container memory cgroup limit exceeded. The 'Task in /docker/<id> killed' message, constraint=CONSTRAINT_MEMCG, and oom_memcg=/docker/... path clearly indicate a cgroup OOM.
action_guide: Increase container memory limit (512m → 1g or higher), or measure peak PIL decoding usage and resize the limit accordingly
For large images, use Pillow's draft() method for reduced decoding or adopt a streaming library like libvips (pyvips)
Explicitly release intermediate objects with del + gc.collect() after processing
Add input image size validation at the application level

accepted_root_causes: Eight worker processes forked via Python multiprocessing.Pool(8) plus the parent = 9 total python processes, each holding ~599MB anon-rss, exceeded the Docker cgroup memory limit of 1GB. Linux fork uses Copy-on-Write so shared pages should be reused, but Python's reference counting modifies the refcount field on every object access (= page write), causing shared pages to be immediately copied and defeating COW. The do_wp_page + mem_cgroup_out_of_memory combination in the Call Trace is definitive evidence of COW collapse — a write-protect fault during which the cgroup memory charge failed.

All python processes in the process_table holding nearly identical RSS (~599MB) is an anomalous pattern. Under normal COW, only the parent should be large while workers remain small. The uniform RSS distribution shows each worker effectively duplicated the parent's entire memory.
must_include_evidence: do_wp_page present in Call Trace (kernel-level evidence of COW breakdown)
process table shows 9 python processes (parent PID 69497 + 8 workers) each holding ~599MB RSS
action_guide: Change multiprocessing start method to 'spawn' or 'forkserver' (explicitly pass data instead of inheriting)
Redesign shared data using multiprocessing.shared_memory, numpy.memmap, or Arrow/Parquet mmap-based files
Reduce worker count (8 → 4) or increase cgroup limit
Store large read-only data as bytes/bytearray before fork (instead of Python lists) — minimizes refcount modifications
Call gc.freeze() to exclude pre-fork objects from GC, reducing refcount modification frequency

accepted_root_causes: The java process (PID 76319) hit the Docker container memory cgroup limit (655360kB = 640MB) and was killed by cgroup OOM. The critical observation is that even with -Xmx512m, the actual anon-rss reached 652544kB (~637MB). This is because -Xmx only bounds the Java Heap, while the total JVM process memory (RSS) = Heap + Metaspace + Code Cache + Thread Stacks + Direct ByteBuffer (off-heap) + JNI native heap. The application allocated Heap ~400MB + Direct ByteBuffer ~210MB, with the Direct ByteBuffer consuming off-heap memory outside the Xmx cap as the root cause.

A classic Kubernetes/Docker JVM misconfiguration pattern where the container memory limit is set equal to or only slightly above Xmx. total-vm:3151064kB (~3GB) being far larger than anon-rss:652544kB reflects the JVM's address space reservation behavior, and since cgroups only account for actual RSS, usage and limit both reached exactly 655360kB.
must_include_evidence: anon-rss (652544kB) > Xmx (524288kB = 512MB): RSS exceeds Xmx by ~125MB
action_guide: Set container memory limit to at least 1.5–2× Xmx (e.g., Xmx 512m → limit 1Gi)
Explicitly set -XX:MaxDirectMemorySize to cap Direct ByteBuffer usage (e.g., -XX:MaxDirectMemorySize=128m)
Pin off-heap region caps with -XX:MaxMetaspaceSize and -XX:ReservedCodeCacheSize
Use -XX:+UseContainerSupport (JDK 8u191+) or -XX:MaxRAMPercentage=75 for container-aware JVM tuning
Enable Native Memory Tracking (-XX:NativeMemoryTracking=summary) and monitor off-heap usage via jcmd <pid> VM.native_memory
Set both Kubernetes pod resources.limits.memory and requests.memory, and align them with JVM options

accepted_root_causes: The java process (PID 83721) exhausted host-wide memory, triggering a kernel-level global OOM kill. constraint=CONSTRAINT_NONE and the global_oom flag confirm this is a system-wide memory shortage, not a specific cgroup limit. anon-rss 6855296kB (~6.5GB) consumed most of the host RAM, and swapents 465760 pages (~1.8GB) shows the 2GB swap was nearly fully consumed. total-vm 14462528kB (~13.8GB) indicates the JVM reserved a large virtual address space per the Xmx=10g setting, which paradoxically prevented an internal heap limit from triggering first and instead caused a kernel OOM.
The process_table listing host-wide processes such as dockerd, containerd, and containerd-shim is a hallmark of global OOM. A cgroup OOM would only show processes within a single container.
must_include_evidence: process_table includes dockerd, containerd, and other host processes
action_guide: Set JVM -Xmx below available host memory (recommended max 80%)
Set a container --memory limit to isolate via cgroup OOM (preventing host-wide impact)
In Kubernetes, explicitly align pod resources.limits.memory with JVM -Xmx
Enable -XX:+UseContainerSupport + -XX:MaxRAMPercentage=75 for automatic calculation
Review host-level vm.overcommit_memory and vm.swappiness tuning

accepted_root_causes: The node process (PID 85760) continuously leaked off-heap native memory via Buffer.alloc(), exhausting the entire host memory. constraint=CONSTRAINT_NONE and the global_oom flag confirm a system-wide OOM. The key cause is that Node.js Buffers are allocated through libuv's native slab allocator rather than the V8 JavaScript heap, so V8 heap limits like --max-old-space-size do not apply. As a result, process.memoryUsage().heapUsed showed almost no change while RSS grew to 6.6GB — a blind spot where internal monitoring tools fail to detect the leak.

anon-rss 6646796kB (~6.3GB) + swapents 443552 pages (~1.7GB) = ~8GB consumed by a single node process. Other processes (dockerd, containerd) combined use under 100MB. A single Node.js process monopolized host memory resources, causing the global OOM.
must_include_evidence: invoked_by=node (Node.js process directly triggered the OOM)
action_guide: Apply LRU or size-based eviction when caching Buffers
Use node --inspect + Chrome DevTools Heap Snapshot to track the 'ArrayBuffer' category (not on the V8 heap)
Monitor process.memoryUsage() rss or external fields instead of heapUsed (Buffers appear under external)
Set a container-level --memory limit to protect the host (isolate via cgroup OOM)
Buffer.allocUnsafe() has the same issue — periodic cache flushing is essential
For large Buffers, use stream-based processing (pipe) or mmap

accepted_root_causes: A gradual memory leak in the python process fully exhausted both the RAM limit (2GB) and the swap limit (1GB), triggering OOM. The decisive evidence is 'swap: usage 1048576kB, limit 1048576kB' showing swap usage == limit (unlike other cgroup OOM cases where swap: usage 0kB, limit 0kB). swap failcnt 5 is the number of times the kernel attempted to push pages to swap but failed, confirming swap saturation. swapents 262016 pages (~1GB) are swap pages held by the killed process. This is a classic memory leak pattern in a long-running server.

memory failcnt 257237 — an extremely large number — is evidence that the kernel retried page allocation over 250,000 times, showing that a gradual leak persisted for a long time before finally triggering. swapcached 761856 bytes indicates the swap cache was actively in use.
action_guide: Trace the memory leak source: use tracemalloc, pympler, or objgraph to compare snapshots after extended runtime
Introduce periodic or graceful restart policies (systemd, supervisord) to prevent leak accumulation
Set --memory-swap more conservatively: --memory-swap=--memory to completely block swap, causing cgroup OOM to trigger earlier for faster detection
For long-running Python servers: use gunicorn --max-requests for periodic worker restarts
Discourage swap usage itself: set vm.swappiness=1 or 0 (prevents sudden container performance degradation)
Set up application-level memory monitoring with Prometheus/Grafana for early leak alerts

accepted_root_causes: numpy's concatenate and sort operations required 2–3× the original data size in peak memory, exceeding the container RAM (2GB) → pages pushed to swap (1GB) → swap also exhausted → OOM. The decisive evidence is the combination of swap: usage 1048576kB == limit + workingset_refault_anon 64602 + pgmajfault 16561, meaning the kernel re-read anonymous pages from swap over 64,000 times (refault) and encountered 16,000+ major page faults. This indicates swap-in was required every time numpy accessed the sort intermediate buffer — a classic thrashing pattern. Understanding numpy's internal behavior (that in-memory sorting 1.5GB of data requires at least 3GB peak) is essential to identifying the root cause.

Unlike case 6 (gradual leak), failcnt is relatively low (8931 vs 257237) but pgmajfault and workingset_refault are much higher — this is the signature distinguishing 'slow leak' vs 'sudden peak burst' patterns. total-vm 3800180kB (~3.6GB) being over 2× anon-rss 1819876kB (~1.7GB) is also evidence of address space reservation for the sort intermediate buffer.
must_include_evidence: thp_fault_alloc 1385 (Transparent Huge Page usage — numpy characteristic)
total-vm:3800180kB vs anon-rss:1819876kB (2× difference — intermediate buffer)
action_guide: Use arr.sort() (in-place) instead of np.sort(arr) to eliminate the temporary buffer, halving peak memory
For large datasets, use numpy.memmap for disk-based processing or dask.array chunking
Use external sort: sort chunks individually then merge with heapq.merge
Size container memory limit to 2–3× peak memory (not 2× working set)
Consider pandas sort_values with kind='mergesort' or polars (more memory-efficient) instead of numpy
Predict peak memory via Prometheus metrics before execution, then dynamically adjust cgroup limit

accepted_root_causes: The postgres query execution backend (PID 210084) hit the Docker container memory cgroup limit (524288kB = 512MB) and was killed by the cgroup OOM Killer. The root cause is that work_mem=2GB is 4× the container memory limit (512MB), and when executing a 3-way JOIN + ORDER BY query, PostgreSQL attempts to allocate sort/hash buffers up to the work_mem size. The Call Trace path filemap_read → __filemap_add_folio → try_charge_memcg → mem_cgroup_out_of_memory shows that the PostgreSQL backend's pread64 syscall for reading data files into page cache failed the cgroup memory charge. failcnt 330081 indicates over 330,000 repeated memory allocation failures, and pgscan_direct 338977 shows the kernel attempted direct reclaim to recover pages but reached its limit.

In the process_table, PID 210084 (query execution backend) has RSS of 562860kB (~550MB), overwhelmingly larger than the other 6 postgres processes (combined ~62KB) + psql (3.5MB), with this single backend monopolizing the entire cgroup memory. anon 512880640 bytes (~489MB) accounts for 93% of total cgroup usage, confirming work_mem buffers were allocated as anonymous memory. shmem 17043456 (~16MB) is the PostgreSQL shared_buffers residual.
must_include_evidence: filemap_read → try_charge_memcg path (pread64 page cache charge failure)
PID 210084 RSS (562860kB) exceeds all other processes combined in process_table
action_guide: Reduce work_mem to fit the container memory limit (recommended: work_mem = container_limit / max_connections / 4, e.g., 512MB/100/4 = 1.3MB)
Increase container memory limit (512MB → 1GB or higher), or ensure work_mem + shared_buffers total does not exceed 80% of the limit
Retune all PostgreSQL memory parameters (shared_buffers, effective_cache_size, maintenance_work_mem) for the container environment
For queries requiring large JOINs/sorts, use LIMIT/OFFSET paging or cursors (DECLARE CURSOR) to reduce peak memory
Monitor memory-intensive queries with pg_stat_activity + EXPLAIN (ANALYZE, BUFFERS)
For PostgreSQL in Kubernetes/Docker: set cgroup-aware options such as huge_pages=off, dynamic_shared_memory_type=posix

accepted_root_causes: redis-server (PID 223055) hit the Docker container memory cgroup limit (1048576kB = 1GB) and was killed by the cgroup OOM Killer. The root cause is that Redis was running without a maxmemory setting (maxmemory=0), meaning no Redis-level memory limit, while a python3 script was bulk-inserting 1500 keys × 1MB each (~1.5GB) via redis-cli --pipe. Redis uses a jemalloc-based memory allocator and stores each SET key-value pair in anonymous memory, so anon 1070288896 bytes (~1020MB) consumed 99% of the entire cgroup. The OOM was invoked by python3 (PID 223275), and the Call Trace path pipe_write → alloc_pages → __memcg_kmem_charge_page → try_charge_memcg → mem_cgroup_out_of_memory shows that python3 failed the cgroup charge when the kernel attempted to allocate pipe buffer memory during a pipe write syscall to redis-cli.

In the process_table, redis-server (PID 223055) RSS of 1040640kB (~1016MB) consumes nearly 100% of the cgroup limit (1GB), while python3 (11.9MB) + redis-cli (2.5MB) are negligible. redis-server monopolizes container memory as a single process. inactive_anon 1070276608 bytes (~1020MB) being nearly the entirety while active_anon is only 12288 bytes suggests Redis is holding a large number of recently unaccessed keys.
must_include_evidence: memory: usage 1048576kB, limit 1048576kB (usage == limit, 1GB fully consumed)
pipe_write present in Call Trace (OOM occurred during python3 → redis-cli pipe communication)
redis-server (PID 223055) RSS (1040640kB) consumes 99% of cgroup in process_table
invoked_by=python3 (OOM was triggered by the data insertion script, not Redis itself)
action_guide: Set Redis maxmemory: must be at most 80% of container memory limit (e.g., --maxmemory 800mb)
Configure maxmemory-policy: enable automatic eviction on memory limit (e.g., allkeys-lru, volatile-lru)
Increase container memory limit or size it to match the data volume
Monitor beforehand with MEMORY USAGE and INFO memory commands during bulk data insertion
Distribute single-instance memory burden with Redis Cluster or sharding
Prevent runtime maxmemory changes via rename-command CONFIG '' in redis.conf + force maxmemory injection in Docker entrypoint

accepted_root_causes: The hog process (PID 224870) ran in a Docker container without a memory limit (--memory not set), exhausting the entire host memory. constraint=CONSTRAINT_NONE and the global_oom flag confirm a system-wide OOM. hog's anon-rss 6225664kB (~5.94GB) + swapents 391872 pages (~1530MB) = ~7.4GB total consumed by a single process, monopolizing nearly all of the host RAM (2014879 pages = ~7.7GB). Free swap = 0kB confirms the 2GB swap was also fully exhausted. all_unreclaimable? yes confirms the kernel had no more reclaimable pages. The OOM was invoked by chronyd (PID 256), a system daemon that failed page allocation during its own filemap_fault handling — not by hog itself but by an unrelated process.

The process_table listing host-wide processes such as dockerd, containerd, node, and claude is a hallmark of global OOM. A cgroup OOM would only show processes within a single container. hog (6225920kB) vs the second-largest process claude (282124kB): hog consumes 20× more memory and was selected due to the highest OOM score.
must_include_evidence: process_table includes dockerd, containerd, chronyd, and other host processes
invoked_by=chronyd (unrelated system daemon triggered the OOM)
action_guide: Always set a Docker container --memory limit to isolate via cgroup OOM (preventing host-wide impact)
Use docker run --memory=2g --memory-swap=2g to specify container memory caps
In Kubernetes, resources.limits.memory is mandatory
Host-level defense: set vm.overcommit_memory=2 to block overcommit (fail at malloc stage)
In C programs, check malloc return value for NULL + implement a proper memory cap
Monitoring: use cAdvisor/Prometheus to track container RSS, with alerts for containers without a memory limit

accepted_root_causes: The mmap_touch process (PID 225398) hit the Docker container memory cgroup limit (524288kB = 512MB) and was killed by cgroup OOM. The program mmap'd an 800MB file with MAP_SHARED and touched every page, then continuously allocated anonymous memory via malloc(10MB)+memset loops. MAP_SHARED mmap pages are file-backed and can be evicted by the kernel under cgroup memory pressure, but anonymous memory (malloc) is non-evictable. Ultimately anon 531910656 bytes (~507MB) filled 99% of the 512MB cgroup. The Call Trace path filemap_fault → __filemap_get_folio → filemap_add_folio → __mem_cgroup_charge → try_charge_memcg → mem_cgroup_out_of_memory shows that a page fault while re-reading a previously evicted mmap file page failed the cgroup charge — because anonymous memory had consumed all available space.

file-rss:0kB in the kill line is the key point. Despite mmap'ing an 800MB file, file-rss is 0 because the kernel had already evicted all file-backed pages just before the OOM Killer ran. pgscan_direct 412340 vs pgsteal_direct 205056 (50% steal ratio) shows the kernel scanned 410,000 pages and reclaimed 200,000 but still fell short. failcnt 212666 represents over 210,000 cgroup charge failures — evidence of repeated page fault → evict → re-fault → evict thrashing cycles on every mmap file access.
must_include_evidence: file-rss:0kB (800MB mmap yet file-rss 0 = kernel evicted all file pages)
filemap_fault → __filemap_add_folio path (cgroup charge failure during mmap file page fault)
pgscan_direct 412340 vs pgsteal_direct 205056 (50% steal ratio — heavy direct reclaim)
action_guide: Understand the distinction between file-backed and anonymous memory when using mmap: MAP_SHARED is file-backed (evictable), MAP_ANONYMOUS/MAP_PRIVATE is anonymous (non-evictable)
For large file processing, use madvise(MADV_SEQUENTIAL) or madvise(MADV_DONTNEED) to hint the kernel to release unneeded pages immediately
When using both mmap and anonymous allocation, ensure the total working set (file-backed + anonymous) fits within the cgroup limit
Size the container memory limit to accommodate the peak working set (file-backed + anonymous + kernel overhead)
For large files, consider using read()/pread() with chunked streaming instead of mmap
Monitor kernel slab overhead if slab_reclaimable 1995040 (~1.9MB) appears abnormally large

accepted_root_causes: A Python fork bomb caused repeated OOM events at the Docker container memory cgroup limit (1048576kB = 1GB). os.fork() infinitely spawned children → each child allocated a 50MB bytearray → cgroup limit reached → OOM Killer killed one child → parent forked again → repeat. The process_table shows 23 python processes each at ~54MB RSS, totaling ~855MB (anon 896933888 ≈ 855MB). kernel 176783360 bytes (~168MB) is abnormally large — this is kernel memory overhead from a massive number of process management structures (task_struct, mm_struct, pagetables, kernel_stack). anon (855MB) + kernel (168MB) ≈ 1023MB reaching the 1GB limit. failcnt 15191708 (15 million) is the result of the continuous fork-kill-fork cycle.

slab_unreclaimable 111534304 bytes (~106MB) comprising 63% of total kernel memory is a fork bomb signature. Under normal containers, slab_unreclaimable is within a few MB, but thousands of fork/exit cycles accumulate task_struct, signal_struct, files_struct, etc. in slab. This kernel memory is not immediately reclaimable even after processes exit (unreclaimable), continuously reducing available memory.
must_include_evidence: process_table shows 23 python processes (fork bomb evidence)
do_anonymous_page present in Call Trace (OOM during child's bytearray page allocation)
action_guide: Set Docker --pids-limit to prevent fork bombs (e.g., --pids-limit=100)
In Kubernetes, add pids limit to spec.containers[].resources.limits
Use multiprocessing.Pool(max_workers=N) instead of os.fork() to limit worker count
Container memory limit already applied to protect host
Set ulimit -u (RLIMIT_NPROC) for a per-user process count cap
Leverage the cgroup pids.max controller (the internal mechanism behind Docker --pids-limit)

accepted_root_causes: The cgroup_stress process (PID 28472) hit the Docker container memory cgroup limit (262144kB = 256MB) and was killed by cgroup OOM. The C program allocated 25 chunks of 10MB each (250MB) via malloc+memset, then OOM occurred on the 26th chunk. anon 267579392 bytes (~255MB) consumed 97% of the cgroup. The Call Trace path filemap_fault → __filemap_get_folio → filemap_add_folio → __mem_cgroup_charge → try_charge_memcg → mem_cgroup_out_of_memory shows that during memset (page touch), the kernel failed the cgroup charge when trying to load the executable's code pages from disk into page cache. Anonymous memory occupied nearly all available space, leaving no room for file page cache allocation.

The most basic cgroup OOM pattern with a single process running in a container. Only cgroup_stress appears in the process_table with RSS 261248kB (~255MB) = 99.6% of the limit. failcnt 308 is relatively low, indicating a clean limit hit without thrashing.
must_include_evidence: filemap_fault → __filemap_add_folio path (OOM during file page cache charge)
Only cgroup_stress single process in process_table
action_guide: Size the container memory limit to 1.2–1.5× the application's peak usage (256MB → 384MB or higher)
In C programs, check malloc return value for NULL + implement a proper allocation cap
Set cgroup v2 memory.high below memory.max to apply throttling first — gradual slowdown instead of abrupt OOM
Configure monitoring so that PSI (Pressure Stall Information) alerts fire at memory.high before memory.max is reached
Consider allowing swap (--memory-swap > --memory) to absorb peak moments into swap
Collect cgroup memory.events max/high/oom counters via Prometheus for proactive alerting

accepted_root_causes: The mysqld process (PID 41882) hit the Docker container memory cgroup limit (409600kB = 400MB) and was killed by the cgroup OOM Killer. anon-rss:403076kB is nearly equal to the limit. The MySQL InnoDB buffer pool size (1GB) greatly exceeds the container limit (400MB), and since mysqld allocates the full innodb_buffer_pool_size as anonymous memory at startup, cgroup OOM occurs. failcnt 27522 indicates repeated memory allocation failures. InnoDB is unaware of the container environment and demands memory according to the configured buffer pool size.
Docker container memory cgroup limit exceeded. constraint=CONSTRAINT_MEMCG and oom_memcg=/docker/06f7fc... path clearly indicate a cgroup OOM.
must_include_evidence: do_anonymous_page present in Call Trace (anonymous memory allocation)
action_guide: Set innodb_buffer_pool_size to 50–70% of the container limit
Use a my.cnf auto-generation script matched to Docker --memory
Leverage MySQL 8's innodb_dedicated_server=ON (automatic detection)

accepted_root_causes: The java process (PID 61470) hit the Docker container memory cgroup limit (409600kB = 400MB) and was killed by the cgroup OOM Killer. anon-rss:327168kB (~320MB). The Elasticsearch JVM heap (-Xmx512m) exceeds the container limit (400MB), and during JVM heap commit the cgroup OOM was triggered. Elasticsearch also uses off-heap memory for Lucene segment file mmap, direct buffers, and thread stacks, so the container limit should be at least 1.5× Xmx.
must_include_evidence: do_anonymous_page present in Call Trace (anonymous memory allocation)
action_guide: Set ES_JAVA_OPTS -Xmx to 50% or less of the container limit
Size the container limit to at least 2× Xmx
Tune Elasticsearch indices.memory.index_buffer_size

accepted_root_causes: The celery process (PID 62378) hit the Docker container memory cgroup limit (524288kB = 512MB) and was killed by the cgroup OOM Killer. anon-rss:515856kB (~504MB) is nearly equal to the limit. The Celery worker's task processing function accumulates 1MB at a time in a global list — a memory leak pattern. As the worker processes more tasks, RSS grows linearly until hitting the cgroup limit. failcnt 675 indicates repeated allocation failures. Python's GC cannot collect objects that are still referenced.
must_include_evidence: do_anonymous_page present in Call Trace (anonymous memory allocation)
action_guide: Set Celery --max-memory-per-child for automatic worker restart
Eliminate global cache usage in tasks, or apply LRU eviction
Trace the leak point with tracemalloc

accepted_root_causes: The goroutine_leak process (PID 48538) hit the Docker container memory cgroup limit (524288kB = 512MB) and was killed by the cgroup OOM Killer. anon-rss:522752kB (~510MB) is nearly equal to the limit. The Go program disabled GC with debug.SetGCPercent(-1) and then repeatedly allocated 50MB byte slices while retaining references in a global slice. With GC disabled, even unused memory cannot be reclaimed. failcnt 112 occurred.
must_include_evidence: do_anonymous_page present in Call Trace (anonymous memory allocation)
action_guide: Set GOGC environment variable or debug.SetGCPercent appropriately (keep the default value of 100)
Use runtime.SetMemoryLimit() for Go 1.19+ soft memory limit
Leverage the GOMEMLIMIT environment variable in container environments

accepted_root_causes: The nginx process (PID 52417) hit the Docker container memory cgroup limit (262144kB = 256MB) and was killed by the cgroup OOM Killer. anon-rss:29532kB. Nginx's proxy_buffers setting (256 × 1MB = 256MB) nearly equals the container limit (256MB), so when buffering large responses, anonymous memory allocation reaches the cgroup limit. failcnt 5551863 indicates extremely severe memory pressure. More concurrent requests lead to sharply increasing buffer memory.
must_include_evidence: do_anonymous_page present in Call Trace (anonymous memory allocation)
action_guide: Set proxy_buffers and proxy_buffer_size conservatively relative to the container limit
Leverage disk-based buffering with proxy_max_temp_file_size
Limit worker_connections to actual requirements

accepted_root_causes: The python3 process (PID 53030) hit the Docker container memory cgroup limit (524288kB = 512MB) and was killed by the cgroup OOM Killer. anon-rss:520712kB (~509MB) is nearly equal to the limit. A leak pattern where Pandas DataFrames are repeatedly created and all references retained in a list. Each DataFrame is ~40MB (100K rows × 50 float64 columns), and after 12 accumulate, ~480MB reaches the 512MB cgroup limit. failcnt 106 occurred.
must_include_evidence: do_anonymous_page present in Call Trace (anonymous memory allocation)
action_guide: Explicitly release unused DataFrames with del df + gc.collect()
Monitor Jupyter kernel memory + set up automatic restart policy
For large datasets, use chunked reads (pd.read_csv chunks) or Dask

accepted_root_causes: The python process (PID 54820) hit the Docker container memory cgroup limit (524288kB = 512MB) and was killed by the cgroup OOM Killer. anon-rss:510752kB (~499MB). TensorFlow Sequential model Dense(8192) layers each allocate an 8192×8192 float32 weight matrix (~256MB). As layers accumulate, model parameter memory exceeds the container limit. failcnt 1098 and pgmajfault 72 occurred. tf.keras allocates all weights into memory immediately upon model construction.
must_include_evidence: do_anonymous_page present in Call Trace (anonymous memory allocation)
action_guide: Size the model to fit container memory (reduce layers, use mixed precision)
Use TF_FORCE_GPU_ALLOW_GROWTH or tf.config.experimental.set_memory_growth
Load model checkpoints in partitions (tf.saved_model)

accepted_root_causes: The vec_explode process (PID 63713) exhausted the entire host memory and was killed by the global OOM Killer. anon-rss:6503808kB (~6.2GB) consumed most of the host RAM (2014879 pages ≈ 7.7GB). constraint=CONSTRAINT_NONE confirms a global OOM. In Rust rayon parallel processing, 8 threads each infinitely expanded a Vec<u8> by 50MB at a time. Running without a --memory limit, the process exhausted the entire host memory. Rust's memory safety (ownership/borrowing) prevents use-after-free but cannot prevent intentional unbounded allocation.
must_include_evidence: __alloc_pages_slowpath present in Call Trace (global OOM path)
do_anonymous_page present in Call Trace (anonymous memory allocation)
action_guide: Always set a Docker container --memory limit
In Rust, configure jemalloc/mimalloc max arena settings for process-level memory caps
Limit thread count with rayon::ThreadPoolBuilder::build

accepted_root_causes: The java process (PID 65790) hit the Docker container memory cgroup limit (524288kB = 512MB) and was killed by the cgroup OOM Killer. anon-rss:521328kB (~509MB) is nearly equal to the limit. The combination of JVM -Xmx4g + -XX:+AlwaysPreTouch exceeds the container limit (512MB). AlwaysPreTouch commits the entire heap to physical memory at JVM startup, causing RSS to spike immediately and trigger cgroup OOM. failcnt 85 occurred.
must_include_evidence: do_anonymous_page present in Call Trace (anonymous memory allocation)
action_guide: Set -Xmx to 70–80% of the container limit
Enable -XX:+UseContainerSupport (JDK 8u191+) for container awareness
Use -XX:MaxRAMPercentage=75 for automatic calculation
Remove -XX:+AlwaysPreTouch or validate it against the container limit

accepted_root_causes: The python3 process (PID 70258) hit the Docker container memory cgroup limit (524288kB = 512MB) and was killed by the cgroup OOM Killer. anon-rss:79368kB. The MongoDB WiredTiger cache size (6GB) exceeds the container limit (512MB) by 12×. After mongod starts and data insertion begins, WiredTiger allocates cache pages, triggering cgroup OOM. failcnt 8460 indicates repeated allocation failures. By default, MongoDB uses 50% of host RAM for the WiredTiger cache, so explicit configuration is required in container environments.
must_include_evidence: do_anonymous_page present in Call Trace (anonymous memory allocation)
action_guide: Set --wiredTigerCacheSizeGB to 40–50% of the container limit
Leverage MongoDB 5.0+ container auto-detection
Use an entrypoint script linked to docker --memory for automatic calculation

accepted_root_causes: The php process (PID 55581) hit the Docker container memory cgroup limit (262144kB = 256MB) and was killed by the cgroup OOM Killer. anon-rss:253044kB (~247MB) is nearly equal to the limit. With PHP memory_limit=-1 (no PHP-level memory limit), the script repeatedly concatenated 50MB strings, causing unbounded memory growth. failcnt 984 and pgmajfault 26 occurred. The php-fpm worker process RSS reached the container limit.
must_include_evidence: do_anonymous_page present in Call Trace (anonymous memory allocation)
action_guide: Set php.ini memory_limit appropriately for the container limit (e.g., 128M)
Use php-fpm pm.max_requests for periodic worker restarts
Explicitly release large variables with unset() in PHP code

accepted_root_causes: The python3 process (PID 81578) exhausted the entire host memory and was killed by the global OOM Killer. anon-rss:6720124kB (~6.4GB) consumed most of the host RAM (2014879 pages ≈ 7.7GB). constraint=CONSTRAINT_NONE confirms a global OOM. Python multiprocessing.Pool(8) with 8 workers each repeatedly allocating 50MB bytearrays. Running without a --memory limit, the 8 parallel processes consumed host memory far faster than a single process.
must_include_evidence: __alloc_pages_slowpath present in Call Trace (global OOM path)
filemap-related function present in Call Trace (page cache path)

accepted_root_causes: The python3 process (PID 55945) exhausted the entire host memory and was killed by the global OOM Killer. anon-rss:6384824kB (~6.1GB) consumed most of the host RAM (2014879 pages ≈ 7.7GB). constraint=CONSTRAINT_NONE confirms a global OOM. Tasks created via asyncio.create_task() never completed (await asyncio.Event().wait()) while each held a 10MB bytearray, accumulating indefinitely. The asyncio event loop retains references to all tasks, so GC cannot reclaim them. Running without a --memory limit, the process exhausted the entire host memory.
must_include_evidence: __alloc_pages_slowpath present in Call Trace (global OOM path)
filemap-related function present in Call Trace (page cache path)
action_guide: Set Docker --memory limit
Limit concurrent task count with asyncio.Semaphore
Release references after consuming completed task results
Use asyncio.timeout() for automatic task cancellation

accepted_root_causes: The python3 process (PID 78321) exhausted both RAM (512MB) and swap (256MB) in a Docker container and was killed by the OOM Killer. anon-rss:520936kB (~509MB). swap: usage 262144kB == limit 262144kB confirms swap is fully exhausted. Repeated 10MB bytearray allocations exceeded RAM → kernel pushed pages to swap → swap also exhausted → OOM at RAM+swap total of 768MB. failcnt 65928 and pgmajfault 152 indicate active swap I/O and sustained memory pressure.
must_include_evidence: swap: usage 262144kB, limit 262144kB (swap == limit, swap exhausted)
filemap-related function present in Call Trace (page cache path)
action_guide: Set --memory-swap equal to --memory to block swap (triggers OOM earlier for faster detection)
Set up process-level memory monitoring to alert before swap usage begins
Set vm.swappiness=1 to minimize swap usage

accepted_root_causes: The python3 process (PID 87219) exhausted both RAM (512MB) and swap (256MB) in a Docker container and was killed by the OOM Killer. anon-rss:402848kB (~393MB). swap: usage 262144kB == limit 262144kB confirms swap is fully exhausted. numpy.sort() allocates a temporary buffer the same size as the original array (600MB), causing peak memory of ~1.2GB. The RAM overshoot was pushed to swap, and swap was also exhausted. failcnt 9762 and pgmajfault 4905 indicate severe thrashing during the sort.
must_include_evidence: swap: usage 262144kB, limit 262144kB (swap == limit, swap exhausted)
do_anonymous_page present in Call Trace (anonymous memory allocation)
action_guide: Use arr.sort() (in-place) instead of np.sort() to eliminate the temporary buffer
For large arrays, use numpy.memmap or dask.array chunking
Size container limit to at least 2× peak memory

accepted_root_causes: The ruby process (PID 59360) exhausted both RAM (512MB) and swap (256MB) in a Docker container and was killed by the OOM Killer. anon-rss:521340kB (~509MB). swap: usage 262144kB == limit 262144kB confirms swap is fully exhausted. The Ruby process simulated a memory leak by repeatedly appending 1MB strings to a global Array. failcnt 65048 and pgmajfault 1070 indicate sustained memory pressure and swap activity. Ruby's GC cannot collect referenced objects, so as the Array grows, GC time increases but memory does not decrease.
must_include_evidence: swap: usage 262144kB, limit 262144kB (swap == limit, swap exhausted)
do_anonymous_page present in Call Trace (anonymous memory allocation)
action_guide: Tune Ruby --jit-max-cache or RUBY_GC_HEAP_INIT_SLOTS
Use Sidekiq's max_rss middleware for automatic worker restarts
Perform periodic ObjectSpace analysis in long-running Ruby processes

accepted_root_causes: The mlx5_core driver failed an order-3 (32KB) contiguous page allocation. Despite free memory existing (Normal: 20480kB free), there were no 32KB contiguous blocks due to memory fragmentation. Zero free blocks at order-3 and above in the buddy allocator is the decisive evidence. The GFP_NORETRY flag prevented the kernel from aggressively attempting compaction/reclaim. active_anon:4021504 pages (~15.3GB) consumed most of the memory.
must_include_evidence: 0*32kB 0*64kB 0*128kB (no contiguous blocks at order-3 and above)
action_guide: Increase vm.min_free_kbytes to reserve more free memory
Trigger manual compaction with vm.compact_memory=1
Disable Transparent Huge Pages (THP) to reduce fragmentation
Update the mlx5_core driver (newer versions support order-0 fallback)

accepted_root_causes: The ZFS z_wr_iss thread failed an order-2 (16KB) page allocation for an L2ARC write buffer. slab_reclaimable at 2097152 pages (~8GB) is extremely high, showing ZFS ARC is holding a massive amount of memory, but ARC shrink is not fast enough, leaving zero order-2+ contiguous blocks in the buddy allocator. All free pages (16384kB) are fragmented into 4kB units (4096*4kB, 0*8kB and above).
must_include_evidence: 0*8kB 0*16kB (no contiguous blocks at order-1 and above)
action_guide: Limit ZFS ARC size: configure the zfs_arc_max parameter
Increase vm.min_free_kbytes (e.g., 524288)
Manually shrink ARC with echo 3 > /proc/sys/vm/drop_caches or arc_prune
Tune the ZFS module parameter zfs_arc_shrink_shift

accepted_root_causes: The vector_oom process (PID 2094) exhausted the entire host memory and was killed by the global OOM Killer. anon-rss:6617672kB (~6.3GB) consumed most of the host RAM (2014879 pages ≈ 7.7GB). constraint=CONSTRAINT_NONE confirms a global OOM. std::vector's push_back reallocates at 2× current capacity when capacity is exceeded (amortized doubling), and during the reallocation the old buffer + new buffer coexist, causing peak memory to spike up to 3×. Unbounded insertion without reserve() and no --memory limit exhausted the entire host memory.
must_include_evidence: __alloc_pages_slowpath present in Call Trace (global OOM path)
filemap-related function present in Call Trace (page cache path)
action_guide: Set Docker --memory limit
Use vector::reserve() for pre-allocation
Use vector::shrink_to_fit() to release excess capacity

accepted_root_causes: The ruby process (PID 7269) hit the Docker container memory cgroup limit (524288kB = 512MB) and was killed by the cgroup OOM Killer. anon-rss:519568kB (~507MB) is nearly equal to the limit. A classic ORM N+1 memory leak pattern: ActiveRecord's all.each converts query results into Ruby objects loaded in memory, and attributes.dup duplicates hashes into a global array. failcnt 253999 indicates extremely severe memory pressure. pgmajfault 88 occurred.
must_include_evidence: filemap-related function present in Call Trace (page cache path)
action_guide: Use find_each(batch_size: 1000) for batch processing
Use includes() to resolve N+1 queries
Eliminate unnecessary attributes duplication

accepted_root_causes: The slice_oom process (PID 8932) exhausted the entire host memory and was killed by the global OOM Killer. anon-rss:6630016kB (~6.3GB) consumed most of the host RAM (2014879 pages ≈ 7.7GB). constraint=CONSTRAINT_NONE confirms a global OOM. Go slice's append doubles capacity when exceeded (growslice). With GC disabled, previous backing arrays were not reclaimed, causing cumulative memory growth. Running without a --memory limit, the process exhausted the entire host memory.
must_include_evidence: __alloc_pages_slowpath present in Call Trace (global OOM path)
filemap-related function present in Call Trace (page cache path)
action_guide: Never disable GC (keep GOGC at the default of 100)
Set a soft limit with runtime.SetMemoryLimit()
For large data, use memory-mapped files or chunked processing instead of slices

accepted_root_causes: The tokio_leak process (PID 11930) hit the Docker container memory cgroup limit (524288kB = 512MB) and was killed by the cgroup OOM Killer. anon-rss:352768kB (~345MB). Async tasks spawned via tokio::spawn never completed (sleep forever) while each held a 10MB Vec<u8>, accumulating indefinitely. The tokio runtime retains JoinHandles for all tasks, preventing drops. failcnt 196 occurred. Rust's ownership system cannot prevent intentional retention.
must_include_evidence: do_anonymous_page present in Call Trace (anonymous memory allocation)
action_guide: Set timeouts on tokio::spawn tasks (tokio::time::timeout)
Limit concurrent task count with Semaphore
Manage task lifecycle with JoinSet

accepted_root_causes: The beam.smp process (PID 12672) hit the Docker container memory cgroup limit (524288kB = 512MB) and was killed by the cgroup OOM Killer. anon-rss:509596kB (~498MB) is nearly equal to the limit. 1MB binary messages accumulated in a BEAM VM GenServer process mailbox faster than they could be processed. While handle_cast took 100ms per message, hundreds of messages queued in the mailbox, each consuming memory. failcnt 64 occurred.
must_include_evidence: do_anonymous_page present in Call Trace (anonymous memory allocation)
action_guide: Implement backpressure in GenServer using the {:continue, ...} pattern
Monitor with Process.info(pid, :message_queue_len)
Build a demand-driven pipeline with GenStage/Flow

accepted_root_causes: The mysqld process (PID 16738) hit the Docker container memory cgroup limit (716800kB ≈ 700MB) and was killed by the cgroup OOM Killer. anon-rss:709852kB (~693MB) is nearly equal to the limit. With max_connections=1000, per-connection buffers (sort_buffer_size + read_buffer_size + join_buffer_size, etc.) are allocated for each concurrent connection. When 800+ connections are active simultaneously, max_connections × per_thread_buffer_sum exceeds the 700MB cgroup limit. failcnt 8284 and pgmajfault 194 occurred.
must_include_evidence: do_anonymous_page present in Call Trace (anonymous memory allocation)
action_guide: Reduce max_connections to fit container memory (e.g., 100–200)
Reduce per-connection buffers (sort_buffer_size, read_buffer_size)
Increase Docker --memory to match MySQL's requirements
Introduce connection pooling to limit concurrent connections

accepted_root_causes: The postgres process (PID 46272) hit the Docker container memory cgroup limit (1048576kB = 1GB) and was killed by the cgroup OOM Killer. anon-rss:47940kB. PostgreSQL shared_buffers=768MB consumes 75% of the 1GB container memory. When a work_mem=256MB query runs concurrently, the combined shared_buffers (shared) + work_mem (per-session) + OS overhead exceeds the 1GB cgroup limit. failcnt 56963 indicates extremely severe memory pressure. The filemap-based Call Trace shows shared_buffers page cache usage.
must_include_evidence: filemap-related function present in Call Trace (page cache path)
action_guide: Set shared_buffers to 25–40% of container memory (250–400MB)
Reduce work_mem to match query characteristics (e.g., 64MB or less)
Verify that effective_cache_size includes OS page cache and does not consume separate memory
Set Docker --memory to at least shared_buffers + work_mem × max_connections + OS overhead

accepted_root_causes: The mongosh process (PID 55621) hit the Docker container memory cgroup limit (524288kB = 512MB) and was killed by the cgroup OOM Killer. anon-rss:83512kB. MongoDB's wiredTigerCacheSizeGB=0.9 exceeds the 512MB container limit. Additionally, the $unwind + $group + $sort aggregation pipeline runs in-memory with allowDiskUse:false, and 10 concurrent pipelines caused memory to spike. failcnt 188 occurred.
must_include_evidence: do_anonymous_page present in Call Trace (anonymous memory allocation)
action_guide: Set wiredTigerCacheSizeGB to (container memory - 1GB) / 2 or minimum 256MB
Change to allowDiskUse: true to enable disk spillover
Add $limit to aggregation pipelines to cap intermediate results
Set Docker --memory to at least WT cache + per-connection memory + OS overhead

accepted_root_causes: The redis-server process (PID 43240) hit the Docker container memory cgroup limit (262144kB = 256MB) and was killed by the cgroup OOM Killer. anon-rss:259620kB (~254MB) is nearly equal to the limit. Redis maxmemory=0 means no memory limit, accepting data without restriction. Loading 200+ keys of 1MB each in a 256MB cgroup container exceeded the limit. SET commands continued without any eviction policy. failcnt 70 occurred.
must_include_evidence: do_anonymous_page present in Call Trace (anonymous memory allocation)
action_guide: Set maxmemory to 70–80% of container memory (e.g., 180MB)
Set maxmemory-policy to allkeys-lru or volatile-lru
Set Docker --memory to at least Redis dataset size + overhead (approximately 1.5×)
When using BGSAVE, account for fork COW overhead and set maxmemory to 50% or less of the container

accepted_root_causes: The pagecache_oom process (PID 52442) exhausted the entire host memory and was killed by the global OOM Killer. anon-rss:6397568kB (~6.1GB) consumed most of the host RAM (2014879 pages ≈ 7.7GB). constraint=CONSTRAINT_NONE confirms a global OOM. Dirty page cache from mmap'd files and anonymous memory allocation via malloc occurred simultaneously, exhausting the entire host memory. 4306 total pagecache pages shows page cache usage. A single process without a --memory limit consumed all host RAM.
must_include_evidence: __alloc_pages_slowpath present in Call Trace (global OOM path)
do_anonymous_page present in Call Trace (anonymous memory allocation)
action_guide: Limit dirty page cache with vm.dirty_ratio / vm.dirty_background_ratio settings
Set Docker --memory limit to prevent a single process from consuming all host memory
Release page cache after use with madvise(MADV_DONTNEED)
Expand swap space or add zram

accepted_root_causes: The shm_oom process (PID 51363) hit the Docker container memory cgroup limit (524288kB = 512MB) and was killed by the cgroup OOM Killer. anon-rss:0kB but shmem-rss:521088kB — System V shared memory segments (shmget/shmat) consumed the cgroup memory. memory: usage 524288kB == limit 524288kB. shmem at ~509MB consumed most of the 512MB container limit. Shared memory is tracked under the shmem account rather than anon-rss, making it difficult to detect with standard RSS monitoring. failcnt 72 occurred.
must_include_evidence: shmem-related function present in Call Trace (tmpfs/shmem path)
action_guide: Reduce shared memory segment size or count
Increase container memory limit after accounting for SysV shm usage
Switch to POSIX shared memory (shm_open) for explicit size control
Monitor shmem usage separately from anon RSS

accepted_root_causes: The dd process (PID 48856) hit the Docker container memory cgroup limit (524288kB = 512MB) and was killed by the cgroup OOM Killer. anon-rss:896kB is very low, but tmpfs writes consumed cgroup memory via the shmem account. memory: usage 524288kB == limit 524288kB. dd performed bulk writes to a tmpfs mount, consuming memory through the shmem_add_to_page_cache path. tmpfs files count against cgroup memory, not as regular page cache. failcnt 1193 and pgmajfault 43 occurred.
must_include_evidence: do_anonymous_page present in Call Trace (anonymous memory allocation)
action_guide: Reduce tmpfs usage or switch to a disk-backed filesystem for large files
Increase container memory limit after accounting for tmpfs usage
Set tmpfs size limit below the container memory limit
Monitor tmpfs/shmem usage as part of the memory budget

accepted_root_causes: The thp_oom process (PID 80662) exhausted the entire host memory and was killed by the global OOM Killer. anon-rss:5952156kB (~5.7GB) consumed most of the host RAM (2014879 pages ≈ 7.7GB). constraint=CONSTRAINT_NONE confirms a global OOM. MADV_HUGEPAGE triggered Transparent Huge Page (THP) allocation, with anon_thp:2899968kB (~2.76GB) of THP in use. THP promotion caused memory fragmentation and host memory exhaustion. 123802 total pagecache pages shows significant page cache consumption. Running without a --memory limit, the process exhausted the entire host memory.

filemap-related function present in Call Trace (page cache path)
action_guide: Disable THP if not needed: echo never > /sys/kernel/mm/transparent_hugepage/enabled
Use madvise mode: enable MADV_HUGEPAGE only for specific workloads

accepted_root_causes: The zombie_storm process (PID 79668) exhausted the entire host memory and was killed by the global OOM Killer. anon-rss:6429184kB (~6.1GB) consumed most of the host RAM (2014879 pages ≈ 7.7GB). constraint=CONSTRAINT_NONE confirms a global OOM. The parent process allocated ~7.6GB without limits, combined with fork storm overhead. zombie_storm exhausted host RAM + swap, then the fork storm created zombie processes. A single process without a --memory limit consumed the entire host memory.

accepted_root_causes: The java process (PID 88716) hit the Docker container memory cgroup limit (1048576kB = 1GB) and was killed by the cgroup OOM Killer. anon-rss:1043348kB (~1019MB) is nearly equal to the limit. A Spring Boot actuator heap dump (jcmd GC.heap_dump) serialized the entire heap to an hprof file, doubling memory usage. Simultaneously, DirectByteBuffer off-heap allocation (Netty NIO buffer simulation) compounded the issue. JVM Xmx=900m in a 1GB container left insufficient headroom for off-heap memory. failcnt 194548 indicates extremely severe memory pressure.

accepted_root_causes: The java process (PID 42648) hit the Docker container memory cgroup limit (1048576kB = 1GB) and was killed by the cgroup OOM Killer. anon-rss:950388kB (~928MB). The Elasticsearch fielddata cache loaded all unique terms from text fields into JVM heap. Without an indices.fielddata.cache.size limit, fielddata grew unbounded, and the JVM heap (Xmx=600m) plus off-heap exceeded the 1GB container limit. With 100K+ unique terms, the fielddata cache consumed hundreds of MB. failcnt 1911 occurred. Two java processes — main ES (PID 42648, RSS ~950MB) and the controller (PID 42545, RSS ~108MB) — shared the available memory.
must_include_evidence: Two java processes: main ES (pid 42648, RSS ~950MB) and controller (pid 42545, RSS ~108MB)
do_anonymous_page present in Call Trace (anonymous memory allocation)
action_guide: Use keyword field type or doc_values instead of fielddata for aggregations
Set indices.fielddata.cache.size (e.g., 20%) to cap fielddata memory
Leverage the indices.breaker.fielddata.limit circuit breaker
Increase container memory or reduce Xmx to leave OS-level headroom

accepted_root_causes: The node process (PID 71386) exhausted the entire host memory and was killed by the global OOM Killer. anon-rss:6560052kB (~6.3GB) consumed most of the host RAM (2014879 pages ≈ 7.7GB). constraint=CONSTRAINT_NONE confirms a global OOM. Node.js V8 code cache created 1.12M unique compiled code objects via repeated new Function() calls, and Buffer.alloc() external memory grew to 7GB+. V8 max-old-space-size=16384 prevented internal OOM, allowing unchecked growth until kernel global OOM. all_unreclaimable? yes confirms no reclaimable memory remained. No --memory limit was set.
must_include_evidence: __alloc_pages_slowpath present in Call Trace (global OOM path)
filemap-related function present in Call Trace (page cache path)
action_guide: Set Docker --memory limit to restrict Node.js memory
Set a reasonable --max-old-space-size (e.g., 512m or 1g instead of 16GB)
Prohibit dynamic code generation (new Function/eval) inside loops
Use worker_threads or process isolation for untrusted code evaluation
Monitor process.memoryUsage().external to detect Buffer leaks

accepted_root_causes: The prometheus process (PID 54721) hit the Docker container memory cgroup limit (262144kB = 256MB) and was killed by the cgroup OOM Killer. anon-rss:245376kB (~240MB). Prometheus TSDB head chunks grew unboundedly due to high-cardinality metric scraping (500K unique time series). Each unique label combination creates a separate in-memory chunk. GOGC=off disabled Go GC, making memory unrecoverable. failcnt 6751725 indicates extreme memory pressure. pgmajfault 245806 shows active page faulting. The filemap-based Call Trace shows TSDB data file access.

action_guide: Reduce metric cardinality (avoid using unique IDs as labels)
Set GOGC to a reasonable value (keep the default of 100); never disable GC
Increase Prometheus container memory limit
Configure --storage.tsdb.head-chunks-write-queue-size and sample limits
Set sample_limit in scrape_configs to reject excessive scrapes
